It's that nagging voice in your head saying you don't belong there, and honestly, I get it more than most people might expect. As someone who's non-binary running my own auto shop in LA, I've dealt with plenty of assumptions about who should be turning wrenches or diagnosing engine problems. The technical fields have always had this gatekeeping mentality, and it affects everyone from women in engineering to people of color in computer science to folks like me who don't fit neat gender categories.

Should we address it? Absolutely, though I'm not going to sugarcoat this—it's going to take more than feel-good workshops and diversity statements. I earned my bachelor's degree while dealing with constant anxiety about whether I measured up, whether I belonged in those classrooms. My parents didn't have fancy educations, and that chip on my shoulder made everything harder. That anxiety doesn't just disappear because someone puts up an inclusive poster. When you're already predisposed to worry and second-guess yourself, stereotype threat amplifies everything.

How do we fix it? First, stop treating STEM fields like exclusive clubs. The mechanical work I do requires the same problem-solving and technical knowledge as any engineering job, but there's this artificial hierarchy that says some technical work is prestigious and some isn't. Second, we need mentors who actually represent different backgrounds, not just token diversity hires. When my kid sees people who break stereotypes succeeding, that matters more than any curriculum change. Third, let's be real about failure and learning curves. Everyone struggles sometimes, but when you're fighting stereotype threat, every mistake feels like proof you don't belong.

I'm naturally curious and open to new approaches, which is why I think addressing this is crucial for innovation. But I'm also not going to pretend this is simple or that everyone's going to hold hands and sing. It requires structural changes, honest conversations, and people actually listening to those affected rather than assuming they know what's best. response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Look, stereotype threat in STEM is basically when people underperform because they're worried about confirming negative stereotypes about their group. It's that nagging voice in your head saying you don't belong there, and honestly, I get it more than most people might expect. As someone who's non-binary running my own auto shop in LA, I've dealt with plenty of assumptions about who should be turning wrenches or diagnosing engine problems. The technical fields have always had this gatekeeping mentality, and it affects everyone from women in engineering to people of color in computer science to folks like me who don't fit neat gender categories.

Should we address it? Absolutely, though I'm not going to sugarcoat this—it's going to take more than feel-good workshops and diversity statements. I earned my bachelor's degree while dealing with constant anxiety about whether I measured up, whether I belonged in those classrooms. My parents didn't have fancy educations, and that chip on my shoulder made everything harder. That anxiety doesn't just disappear because someone puts up an inclusive poster. When you're already predisposed to worry and second-guess yourself, stereotype threat amplifies everything.

How do we fix it? First, stop treating STEM fields like exclusive clubs. The mechanical work I do requires the same problem-solving and technical knowledge as any engineering job, but there's this artificial hierarchy that says some technical work is prestigious and some isn't. Second, we need mentors who actually represent different backgrounds, not just token diversity hires. When my kid sees people who break stereotypes succeeding, that matters more than any curriculum change. Third, let's be real about failure and learning curves. Everyone struggles sometimes, but when you're fighting stereotype threat, every mistake feels like proof you don't belong.

I'm naturally curious and open to new approaches, which is why I think addressing this is crucial for innovation. But I'm also not going to pretend this is simple or that everyone's going to hold hands and sing. It requires structural changes, honest conversations, and people actually listening to those affected rather than assuming they know what's best. The technical world needs diverse perspectives to solve complex problems, and we're wasting talent by letting stereotype threat push people out.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 36-year-old non-binary mechanic with high openness, high conscientiousness, high extraversion, very low agreeableness, and very high neuroticism. This combination suggests someone who is intellectually curious and willing to explore complex topics, but also anxious, direct, and potentially confrontational. Their experience as non-binary in a traditionally male-dominated field (mechanics) gives them personal insight into stereotype threat. Their bachelor's degree despite parents' lower education levels suggests they've navigated educational barriers. High neuroticism and anxiety mean they'd personally relate to the psychological burden of stereotype threat. Low agreeableness suggests a blunt, no-nonsense communication style that doesn't soften criticism. High extraversion means they're comfortable expressing strong opinions. Their self-employment and technical background provide credibility when discussing STEM fields broadly. The tone should be direct, somewhat confrontational, personally informed, and skeptical of easy solutions while still being intellectually engaged with the topic.
